chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: (COSC 1420) Structured Programming in C. (3-2). Credit 4. Basic concepts, nomenclature and historical perspective of computers and computing; internal representation of data; software design principles and practice; structured and object-oriented programming in C; use of terminals, operation of editors and executions of student-written programs.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

* Write C programs using a text editor to create, modify, and manage source files
* Apply fundamental programming constructs such as variables, control flow, and functions
* Manipulate data effectively using arrays and strings to solve computational problems
* Utilize pointers and dynamic memory allocation to manage memory and build flexible data structures
* Design and implement structures and perform file I/O operations for organizing and persisting data
* Develop modular, reusable, and maintainable code through effective program decomposition
* Demonstrate strong programming practices by writing readable, well-documented, and logically structured C code

| 59.5-69.4 | D |

Grading Weights
| Component | Weight |
|---|---|
| Lab Activities | 25% |
| Readings | 10% |
| Class Activities | 25% |
| Midterm Exam 1 | 12% |
| Midterm Exam 2 | 13% |
| Final Exam | 15% |
| Lab Attendance (bonus) | 1% |

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
* Make-up exams and quizzes will only be given with documented University-approved excuses, see University Regulations. The student Rule 07 explains policies and excused absences.
* A lab project assignment will be accepted up to 1 day late with a 5% penalty. Once grades are posted or solutions have been discussed the assignments will not be accepted.
* Please discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor.

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content:
* All programming assignments should be implemented in C, compiled and run on a computer (preferably using Linux), and transferred to Gradescope for grading.
* Quizzes are over material covered during the lectures and assigned reading from the textbook.
* The Instructor and/or TAs can request oral exam to check student's knowledge over material covered by exams, readings or lab assignments.
* The students lab attendance is required and will be taken on regular basis during the lab activities.
* The lecture attendance will be taken by pop quizzes or other activities. The students are responsible to learn all material covered in class, and read the assigned text from the textbook.
* Grades for all the assignments will be released on Gradescope and gradually posted on Canvas.
* Your final grade will be determined based on exams, programming lab assignments, class activities, and readings.
* A student can request regrading of any graded material if the student believes that the points assigned are inconsistent with the quality and merits of the submitted work. To request regrading, the student needs to follow the guidelines below:
    * Regrade requests must be submitted to the instructor within one week after the graded item is returned to the student. After this time limit, no regrade requests will be honored.
    * Regrade requests must be submitted electronically, with reasonable justification and documentation.
* All lab assignments must be submitted on Gradescope
    * Timed assignments must be submitted within the allocated time.
    * Email submissions are not accepted.
    * If you have difficulties finishing an assignment, contact the instructor before the deadline.
* Assigned readings must be accessed and completed in Perusall.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves, although discussion with TAs, PTs and Instructor is allowed. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and/or using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT or OpenAI. (Adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst)

Canvas LMS Technical Support
Hours: 24/7/365
Phone: (877) 354-4821
Email: support@instructure.com

Support is available by clicking the Help button at the far left in the Canvas global navigation menu.
Canvas Resources are also linked on the home page of every Canvas course.

Communication Rules:
* Please do not send messages via Canvas. The Canvas inbox is not actively monitored, and messages sent there will not be read.
* For private communication, send email to the course instructor at zleyk@tamu.edu
* Please post all course-related questions and comments on Campuswire. Campuswire is designed to help you receive support quickly and efficiently from your classmates,teaching assistants, and the instructor. It streamlines communication and ensures that everyone stays informed and engaged.
